name: pyquery
documentation: # pyquery: a jquery-like library for python ```{image} https://github.com/gawel/pyquery/actions/workflows/tox.yml/badge.svg :alt: Build Status :target: https://github.com/gawel/pyquery/actions/workflows/tox.yml ``` pyquery allows you to make jquery queries on xml documents. The API is as much as possible similar to jquery. pyquery uses lxml for fast xml and html manipulation. This is not (or at least not yet) a library to produce or interact with javascript code. I just liked the jquery API and I missed it in python so I told myself "Hey let's make jquery in python". This is the result. The [project] is being actively developed on a git repository on Github. I have the policy of giving push access to anyone who wants it and then reviewing what they do. So if you want to contribute just email me. Please report bugs on the [github](https://github.com/gawel/pyquery/issues) issue tracker. % >>> (urlopen, your_url, path_to_html_file) = getfixture('readme_fixt') # Quickstart You can use the PyQuery class to load an xml document from a string, a lxml document, from a file or from an url: ``` >>> from pyquery import PyQuery as pq >>> from lxml import etree >>> import urllib >>> d = pq("") >>> d = pq(etree.fromstring("")) >>> d = pq(url=your_url) >>> d = pq(url=your_url, ... opener=lambda url, **kw: urlopen(url).read()) >>> d = pq(filename=path_to_html_file) ``` Now d is like the \$ in jquery: ``` >>> d("#hello") [] >>> p = d("#hello") >>> print(p.html()) Hello world ! >>> p.html("you know Python rocks") [] >>> print(p.html()) you know Python rocks >>> print(p.text()) you know Python rocks ``` You can use some of the pseudo classes that are available in jQuery but that are not standard in css such as :first :last :even :odd :eq :lt :gt :checked \:selected :file: ``` >>> d('p:first') [] ``` See for the full documentation # News ## 2.0.1 (2024-08-30) - Breaking change: its seems no longer possible to use the html parser with a xml file so its no longer tested - Drop support for python 3.7 ## 2.0.0 (2022-12-28) - Breaking change: inputs starting with `"http://"` or `"https://"` like `PyQuery("http://example.com")` will no longer fetch the contents of the URL. Users desiring the old behavior should switch to `PyQuery(url="http://example.com")`. - Add nextUntil method - `.remove()` no longer inserts a space in place of the removed element - Fix escaping of top-level element text in `.html()` output - Support (and require) cssselect 1.2+ - Drop support for python 3.5/3.6 ## 1.4.3 (2020-11-21) - No longer use a universal wheel ## 1.4.2 (2020-11-21) - Fix exception raised when calling `PyQuery("").text()` - python2 is no longer supported ## 1.4.1 (2019-10-26) - This is the latest release with py2 support - Remove py33, py34 support - web scraping improvements: default timeout and session support - Add API methods to serialize form-related elements according to spec - Include HTML markup when querying textarea text/value ## 1.4.0 (2018-01-11) - Refactoring of `.text()` to match firefox behavior. ## 1.3.0 (2017-10-21) - Remove some unmaintained modules: `pyquery.ajax` and `pyquery.rules` - Code cleanup. No longer use ugly hacks required by python2.6/python3.2. - Run tests with python3.6 on CI - Add a `method` argument to `.outer_html()` ## 1.2.17 (2016-10-14) - `PyQuery('').val()` is `''` - `PyQuery('').val()` is `''` ## 1.2.16 (2016-10-14) - `.attr('value', '')` no longer removes the `value` attribute - `` without `value="..."` have a `.val()` of `'on'` - `` without `value="..."` have a `.val()` of `'on'` - `

methods.name: pyquery.cssselectpatch.JQueryTranslator.xpath_selected_pseudo
documentation:
Matches all elements that are selected:

```
>>> from pyquery import PyQuery
>>> d = PyQuery('<select><option selected="selected"/></select>')
>>> d('option:selected')
[<option>]
```

%

methods.name: pyquery.cssselectpatch.JQueryTranslator._format_disabled_xpath
documentation:
Format XPath condition for :disabled or :enabled pseudo-classes
according to the WHATWG spec. See: https://html.spec.whatwg.org
/multipage/semantics-other.html#concept-element-disabled

methods.name: pyquery.cssselectpatch.JQueryTranslator.xpath_has_function
documentation:
Matches elements which contain at least one element that matches the specified selector. https://api.jquery.com/has-selector/ >>> from pyquery import PyQuery >>> d = PyQuery('
') >>> d('.foo:has(".baz")') [] >>> d('.foo:has(".foo")') [] >>> d('.foo:has(".bar")') [] >>> d('.foo:has(div)') [] ..

This class is used to implement the css pseudo classes
(:first, :last, ...) that are not defined in the css standard,
but are defined in the jquery API.
keep cssselect < 0.8 compat for now

methods.name: pyquery.pyquery.PyQuery.items
documentation:
Iter over elements. Return PyQuery objects: ```pycon >>> d = PyQuery('
foobar
') >>> [i.text() for i in d.items('span')] ['foo', 'bar'] >>> [i.text() for i in d('span').items()] ['foo', 'bar'] >>> list(d.items('a')) == list(d('a').items()) True ```

methods.name: pyquery.pyquery.PyQuery.xhtml_to_html
documentation:
Remove xhtml namespace:

```pycon
>>> doc = PyQuery(
...         '<html xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1999/xhtml"></html>')
>>> doc
[<{http://www.w3.org/1999/xhtml}html>]
>>> doc.xhtml_to_html()
[<html>]
```

methods.name: pyquery.pyquery.PyQuery.remove_namespaces
documentation:
Remove all namespaces:

```pycon
>>> doc = PyQuery('<foo xmlns="http://example.com/foo"></foo>')
>>> doc
[<{http://example.com/foo}foo>]
>>> doc.remove_namespaces()
[<foo>]
```

\>>> print(str(xml))

methods.name: pyquery.pyquery.PyQuery.filter
documentation:
Filter elements in self using selector (string or function): ```pycon >>> d = PyQuery('

Hi

Bye

') >>> d('p') [,

] >>> d('p').filter('.hello') [] >>> d('p').filter(lambda i: i == 1) [

] >>> d('p').filter(lambda i: PyQuery(this).text() == 'Hi') [] >>> d('p').filter(lambda i, this: PyQuery(this).text() == 'Hi') [] ```

methods.name: pyquery.pyquery.PyQuery.not_
documentation:
Return elements that don't match the given selector: ```pycon >>> d = PyQuery('

Hi

Bye

') >>> d('p').not_('.hello') [

] ```

methods.name: pyquery.pyquery.PyQuery.is_
documentation:
Returns True if selector matches at least one current element, else False: >>> d = PyQuery('

Hi

Bye

') >>> d('p').eq(0).is_('.hello') True >>> d('p').eq(0).is_('span') False >>> d('p').eq(1).is_('.hello') False ..

methods.name: pyquery.pyquery.PyQuery.find
documentation:
Find elements using selector traversing down from self: ```pycon >>> m = '

Whoah!

there

' >>> d = PyQuery(m) >>> d('p').find('em') [, ] >>> d('p').eq(1).find('em') [] ```

methods.name: pyquery.pyquery.PyQuery.eq
documentation:
Return PyQuery of only the element with the provided index: ``` >>> d = PyQuery('

Hi

Bye

') >>> d('p').eq(0) [] >>> d('p').eq(1) [

] >>> d('p').eq(2) [] ``` %

methods.name: pyquery.pyquery.PyQuery.each
documentation: apply func on each nodes

methods.name: pyquery.pyquery.PyQuery.map
documentation:
Returns a new PyQuery after transforming current items with func. func should take two arguments - 'index' and 'element'. Elements can also be referred to as 'this' inside of func: ``` >>> d = PyQuery('

Hi there

Bye


') >>> d('p').map(lambda i, e: PyQuery(e).text()) ['Hi there', 'Bye'] >>> d('p').map(lambda i, e: len(PyQuery(this).text())) [8, 3] >>> d('p').map(lambda i, e: PyQuery(this).text().split()) ['Hi', 'there', 'Bye'] ```

methods.name: pyquery.pyquery.PyQuery.end
documentation:
Break out of a level of traversal and return to the parent level. ```pycon >>> m = '

Whoah!

there

' >>> d = PyQuery(m) >>> d('p').eq(1).find('em').end().end() [

,

] ```

methods.name: pyquery.pyquery.PyQuery.hide
documentation:
Add display:none to elements style:

```pycon
>>> print(PyQuery('<div style="display:none;"/>').hide())
<div style="display: none"/>
```
CORE UI EFFECTS #

methods.name: pyquery.pyquery.PyQuery.show
documentation:
Add display:block to elements style:

```pycon
>>> print(PyQuery('<div />').show())
<div style="display: block"/>
```

methods.name: pyquery.pyquery.PyQuery.val
documentation:
Set the attribute value:

```
>>> d = PyQuery('<input />')
>>> d.val('Youhou')
[<input>]
```

Get the attribute value:

```
>>> d.val()
'Youhou'
```

Set the selected values for a `select` element with the `multiple`
attribute:

```
>>> d = PyQuery('''
...             <select multiple>
...                 <option value="you"><option value="hou">
...             </select>
...             ''')
>>> d.val(['you', 'hou'])
[<select>]
```

Get the selected values for a `select` element with the `multiple`
attribute:

```
>>> d.val()
['you', 'hou']
```
HTML #

methods.name: pyquery.pyquery.PyQuery.html
documentation:
Get or set the html representation of sub nodes. Get the text value: ``` >>> d = PyQuery('
toto
') >>> print(d.html()) toto ``` Extra args are passed to `lxml.etree.tostring`: ``` >>> d = PyQuery('
') >>> print(d.html()) >>> print(d.html(method='html')) ``` Set the text value: ``` >>> d.html('Youhou !') [
] >>> print(d)
Youhou !
```

methods.name: pyquery.pyquery.PyQuery.outer_html
documentation:
Get the html representation of the first selected element: ``` >>> d = PyQuery('
toto rocks
') >>> print(d('span')) toto rocks >>> print(d('span').outer_html()) toto >>> print(d('span').outerHtml()) toto >>> S = PyQuery('

Only me & myself

') >>> print(S('b').outer_html()) me ``` %

methods.name: pyquery.pyquery.PyQuery.text
documentation:
Get or set the text representation of sub nodes. Get the text value: ``` >>> doc = PyQuery('
tototata
') >>> print(doc.text()) tototata >>> doc = PyQuery('''
toto ... tata
''') >>> print(doc.text()) toto tata ``` Get the text value, without squashing newlines: ``` >>> doc = PyQuery('''
toto ... tata
''') >>> print(doc.text(squash_space=False)) toto tata ``` Set the text value: ``` >>> doc.text('Youhou !') [
] >>> print(doc)
Youhou !
```

methods.name: pyquery.pyquery.PyQuery.wrap
documentation:
A string of HTML that will be created on the fly and wrapped around each target: >>> d = PyQuery('youhou') >>> d.wrap('
') [
] >>> print(d)
youhou

methods.name: pyquery.pyquery.PyQuery.wrap_all
documentation:
Wrap all the elements in the matched set into a single wrapper element: ``` >>> d = PyQuery('
Heyyou !
') >>> print(d('span').wrap_all('
'))
Heyyou !
>>> d = PyQuery('
Heyyou !
') >>> print(d('span').wrapAll('
'))
Heyyou !
``` %

methods.name: pyquery.pyquery.PyQuery.replace_with
documentation:
replace nodes by value:

```pycon
>>> doc = PyQuery("<html><div /></html>")
>>> node = PyQuery("<span />")
>>> child = doc.find('div')
>>> child.replace_with(node)
[<div>]
>>> print(doc)
<html><span/></html>
```

methods.name: pyquery.pyquery.PyQuery.remove
documentation:
Remove nodes: ```pycon >>> h = ( ... '
Maybe she does NOT know
' ... ) >>> d = PyQuery(h) >>> d('strong').remove() [] >>> print(d)
Maybe she does know
```

methods.name: pyquery.pyquery.PyQuery.serialize_array
documentation:
Serialize form elements as an array of dictionaries, whose structure
mirrors that produced by the jQuery API. Notably, it does not handle
the deprecated `keygen` form element.

    >>> d = PyQuery('<form><input name="order" value="spam"></form>')
    >>> d.serialize_array() == [{'name': 'order', 'value': 'spam'}]
    True
    >>> d.serializeArray() == [{'name': 'order', 'value': 'spam'}]
    True
AJAX #

methods.name: pyquery.pyquery.PyQuery.serialize
documentation:
Serialize form elements as a URL-encoded string.

```pycon
>>> h = (
... '<form><input name="order" value="spam">'
... '<input name="order2" value="baked beans"></form>'
... )
>>> d = PyQuery(h)
>>> d.serialize()
'order=spam&order2=baked%20beans'
```

methods.name: pyquery.pyquery.PyQuery.serialize_pairs
documentation:
Serialize form elements as an array of 2-tuples conventional for
typical URL-parsing operations in Python.

    >>> d = PyQuery('<form><input name="order" value="spam"></form>')
    >>> d.serialize_pairs()
    [('order', 'spam')]
    >>> d.serializePairs()
    [('order', 'spam')]
Additional methods that are not in the jQuery API #

methods.name: pyquery.pyquery.PyQuery.serialize_dict
documentation:
Serialize form elements as an ordered dictionary. Multiple values
corresponding to the same input name are concatenated into one list.

    >>> d = PyQuery('''<form>
    ...             <input name="order" value="spam">
    ...             <input name="order" value="eggs">
    ...             <input name="order2" value="ham">
    ...             </form>''')
    >>> d.serialize_dict()
    OrderedDict({'order': ['spam', 'eggs'], 'order2': 'ham'})
    >>> d.serializeDict()
    OrderedDict({'order': ['spam', 'eggs'], 'order2': 'ham'})

methods.name: pyquery.pyquery.PyQuery.base_url
documentation: Return the url of current html document or None if not available.

documentation: Make all links absolute.

inner_classes.name: pyquery.pyquery.PyQuery.Fn
documentation:
Hook for defining custom function (like the jQuery.fn): ```python >>> fn = lambda: this.map(lambda i, el: PyQuery(this).outerHtml()) >>> PyQuery.fn.listOuterHtml = fn >>> S = PyQuery( ... '
  1. Coffee
  2. Tea
  3. Milk
') >>> S('li').listOuterHtml() ['
  • Coffee
    • ', '
  • Tea
    • ', '
  • Milk
    • '] ```
The main class
